40107183.background: EPA and DHA are long-chain omega-3 fatty acids found in different lipid forms in foods and supplements, including triglycerides (TAG), phosphatidylcholine (PC), and lyso-phosphatidylcholine (LPC) — the predominant form in krill oil. Whether the lipid carrier form meaningfully alters the systemic kinetics of EPA and DHA absorption and disposition was not well characterised. This study aimed to compare the single-dose pharmacokinetics of radiolabelled EPA and DHA across these three lipid classes and to develop compartmental models applicable to both radioactive and stable isotope tracers.
40107183.design: Male rats received [1-14C]-radiolabelled EPA or DHA by oral gavage as TAG, PC, or LPC; LPC was also administered intravenously. Sample size not explicitly stated in the abstract. Plasma and whole blood concentration-time profiles were measured from 0 to 168 hours; cumulative radioactivity in expired air, faeces, and urine was tracked for up to 336 hours. Both non-compartmental analysis and compartmental modelling were applied. Models were additionally validated against non-radiolabelled EPA, DPA, and DHA quantified by LC-MS/MS.
40107183.findings: Both EPA and DHA displayed overall first-order kinetics with comparable terminal half-lives across lipid forms. DHA had a significantly lower Cmax than EPA (0.18 ± 0.089 vs. 0.24 ± 0.103 (µg-eq/g)/(mg/kg); P < 0.0001). Between TAG and PC, only Tmax differed significantly (PC: 3.23 ± 0.94 h vs. TAG: 2.55 ± 0.77 h; P = 0.0004). LPC differed significantly from both TAG and PC in AUC0-inf, Cmax, Tmax, and total clearance. TAG and PC showed evidence of some faecal loss without full systemic absorption (early-phase deviation from first-order behaviour), whereas LPC did not. Compartmental models performed equally well for both fatty acids regardless of lipid form and were successfully extended to non-radiolabelled tracers and multiple species.
40107183.limitations: Study conducted exclusively in male rats, limiting direct extrapolation to humans or other species without adaptation. Sample size per group is not reported in the abstract, precluding a full assessment of statistical power. Only a single dose level was used per lipid form, so dose-response kinetics are not characterised. The radiolabel ([1-14C]) tracks the intact fatty acid carbon skeleton but cannot distinguish metabolic remodelling into specific lipid pools. As an animal pharmacokinetic study using radiotracer methodology, evidence quality is moderate; no blinding is relevant in this context, but the absence of female animals and human data are notable constraints.

39169540.background: Marine omega-3 fatty acids and phospholipids have individually demonstrated favorable effects on skin barrier function. Krill oil delivers omega-3 fatty acids in phospholipid form, a combination hypothesized to enhance efficacy for skin health. These pilot studies were conducted to investigate whether krill oil supplementation could improve transepidermal water loss (TEWL) — a key marker of skin barrier integrity — as well as hydration and elasticity in healthy adults.
39169540.design: Two separate randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled pilot studies in healthy adults with a baseline TEWL of >10 and ≤24.9 g/m²/h. Study 1: n=51, 1 g krill oil or placebo daily. Study 2: n=50, 2 g krill oil or placebo daily. Outcomes (TEWL, skin hydration, elasticity, and omega-3 index) were assessed at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks.
39169540.findings: In both studies, krill oil groups showed statistically significant increases in the omega-3 index versus placebo. Significant beneficial reductions in TEWL were observed: Study 1 saw TEWL decrease from 14.47 ± 3.65 to 13.83 ± 3.78 g/m²/h; Study 2 saw a larger reduction from 14.25 ± 3.21 to 13.02 ± 2.76 g/m²/h. Both doses also produced statistically significant improvements in skin hydration and elasticity versus placebo. Significant linear relationships were found between changes in the omega-3 index and changes in all three skin outcomes in both studies, suggesting a dose-dependent effect.
39169540.limitations: Both studies are pilot studies with relatively small sample sizes (n=51 and n=50), limiting statistical power and generalizability. Participants were restricted to a specific TEWL range, so findings may not apply to those with very dry or very healthy skin. The studies were conducted in healthy adults only, limiting applicability to clinical skin conditions. No specific p-values or effect sizes beyond means and standard deviations are reported in the abstract. As pilot studies, replication in larger trials is needed to confirm dose-dependent conclusions. Quality read: moderate — double-blind RCT design is a strength, but small sample sizes and pilot status are notable caveats.

39064695.background: Neonatal hypoxic-ischemic (HI) brain injury is a leading cause of neurological morbidity in newborns, and new therapeutic strategies are urgently needed. Both n-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (n-3 LCPUFAs) and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) have shown neuroprotective and neuroregenerative potential in animal models of neonatal HI. Lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC)-bound n-3 LCPUFAs are known to enhance brain incorporation of these fatty acids, but their effect on HI brain injury had not previously been investigated. This study aimed to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of LPC-n-3 LCPUFAs (as Lysoveta) and whether they could augment the benefits of MSC therapy.
39064695.design: Mouse model study using 9-day-old C57BL/6 mice with surgically induced HI brain injury. Sample size not stated in the abstract. Intervention: oral Lysoveta (LPC-n-3 LCPUFA supplement) administered for 7 days post-HI, with or without intranasal MSC delivery at 3 days post-HI. Functional outcomes were assessed at 21–28 days post-HI via cylinder rearing, novel object recognition, and open field tasks. Brain tissue was assessed for gray matter (MAP2) and white matter (MBP) injury. An in vitro component tested Lysoveta on SH-SY5Y neurons under oxidative stress. Specific doses of Lysoveta and MSCs are not stated in the abstract.
39064695.findings: Oral Lysoveta supplementation reduced gray and white matter brain injury following neonatal HI, as measured by MAP2 and MBP markers. However, it did not ameliorate functional deficits on any of the three behavioural tasks. Lysoveta did not enhance or augment the therapeutic efficacy of MSC therapy when the two were combined. In vitro, Lysoveta protected SH-SY5Y neurons against oxidative stress, suggesting a potential mechanism of action via mitigation of oxidative stress injury. No specific quantitative effect sizes or p-values are reported in the abstract.
39064695.limitations: The study is a preclinical animal model; findings may not translate directly to human neonates. Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. Doses of Lysoveta and MSCs are not specified in the abstract. While structural neuroprotection (gray/white matter) was observed, the absence of functional improvement raises questions about the clinical relevance of the tissue-level findings. The combination of Lysoveta and MSC therapy showed no additive benefit, which may reflect ceiling effects, timing issues, or mechanistic incompatibility not fully explored here. Study quality: preclinical controlled animal study with in vitro component; blinding and randomisation procedures are not described in the abstract.

39057406.background: Mesopelagic fish (deep-sea species such as Maurolicus muelleri and Benthosema glaciale) represent an underexplored marine protein source. This study sought to generate bioactive peptides from these fish via enzymatic hydrolysis and autolysis, then identify sequences with anti-inflammatory, anti-type 2 diabetes (DPP-IV inhibitory), and Umami flavour potential, with a view to developing novel functional foods or topical therapeutic agents.
39057406.design: Species: Maurolicus muelleri and Benthosema glaciale (mesopelagic fish). Intervention: protein hydrolysates generated with the enzyme Alcalase and via autolysis. Bioactivity screening used in vitro assays (COX-1, COX-2, DPP-IV, antioxidant). Active hydrolysates were characterised by mass spectrometry; peptide sequences were ranked using in silico tools (Peptide Ranker, PrepAIP, Umami-MRNN, AntiDMPpred). Seven top-ranked peptides were chemically synthesised and re-tested in vitro. Sample size (n) and study duration are not stated in the abstract. No human or animal subjects; bench/laboratory design.
39057406.findings: Seven synthesised peptides showed anti-inflammatory, DPP-IV inhibitory, or Umami properties. The lead peptide QCPLHRPWAL inhibited COX-1 by 82.90% (±0.54) and COX-2 by 53.84%, and achieved a selectivity index greater than 10, indicating preferential COX-1 selectivity. Additional peptides with DPP-IV inhibitory activity and Umami flavour characteristics were identified, though specific inhibition values for these are not reported in the abstract. Authors highlight QCPLHRPWAL as a candidate for further research as a novel anti-inflammatory/pain-relief agent.
39057406.limitations: Laboratory (in vitro/in silico) study only — no cell-based, animal, or human data reported, limiting translational conclusions. Sample size (number of replicates or biological batches) is not stated in the abstract. No blinding or control arm described. The combined in silico–in vitro pipeline may introduce selection bias toward predicted actives. Bioavailability, stability, and safety of the identified peptides remain untested. Overall quality read: exploratory, hypothesis-generating bench study; findings require validation in more complex biological models before therapeutic claims can be made.

39009356.background: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) are known to regulate inflammation and lipid metabolism, suggesting potential relevance to systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an autoimmune disease characterised by chronic inflammation. This study aimed to determine how frequently omega-3 deficiency occurs in SLE patients and whether krill oil concentrate (KOC) supplementation could correct that deficiency and reduce disease activity.
39009356.design: Multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in adult patients with active SLE. A total of 78 eligible patients were randomised (n=39 per group) to receive either 4 g/day KOC or a placebo (vegetable oil mixture) for 24 weeks, followed by an optional open-label extension to week 48. Primary endpoint was change in red blood cell Omega-3 Index from baseline to week 24; secondary monitoring included SLEDAI-2K disease activity scores.
39009356.findings: The mean baseline Omega-3 Index across the SLE cohort was 4.43% (±1.04%), indicating widespread deficiency. In the KOC group, the index rose rapidly to 7.17% (±1.48%) at week 4 and 8.05% (±1.79%) at week 24 (both p<0.001 vs baseline), with no significant change in the placebo group. Gains persisted through week 48. Patients switching from placebo to KOC at week 24 also showed a significant rise (4.63% to 7.50%; p<0.001). Overall disease activity did not change significantly; however, in the high-disease-activity subgroup (SLEDAI-2K ≥9, n=9 per group), SLEDAI-2K scores decreased significantly in KOC-treated patients vs placebo at weeks 4, 8, and 16 (p=0.04, 0.02, and 0.01, respectively). KOC was well-tolerated with no significant safety concerns.
39009356.limitations: The overall sample size was modest (n=78 randomised; subgroup analyses based on n=9 per group), substantially limiting statistical power for disease activity endpoints. Participant numbers declined over time (e.g., n=25 in KOC arm at week 24), suggesting attrition. The key disease-activity finding is restricted to a post-hoc high-disease-activity subgroup, reducing its inferential strength. Duration of the blinded phase was 24 weeks; longer-term effects rely on an uncontrolled open-label extension. Quality read: well-designed RCT with double-blinding, but underpowered for clinical endpoints and subgroup results should be considered exploratory.

38701928.background: Southern Hemisphere humpback whale (SHHW) breeding populations feed on Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) across distinct sectors of the Southern Ocean. Their capital-breeding life history depends on predictable ecosystem productivity to fuel migration. Climate change is altering Southern Ocean productivity unevenly, so populations feeding in the most impacted areas were hypothesised to be the first to show a departure from a high-fidelity krill diet. This study tested that hypothesis across five SHHW populations.
38701928.design: Population-level observational study comparing five Southern Hemisphere humpback whale breeding populations sampled in 2019. Blubber fatty acid profiles and skin stable isotopes (δ13C and δ15N) were collected from whales, and Antarctic krill stable isotopes were sampled in three SHHW feeding areas of the Southern Ocean, also in 2019. Sample sizes per population or overall n are not reported in the abstract. No intervention or experimental dose; cross-sectional/correlational design.
38701928.findings: Fatty acid profiles and δ13C and δ15N differed significantly among all five populations; however, calculated trophic positions did not differ significantly, ranging narrowly from 2.7 to 3.1. Fatty acid ratios (16:1ω7c/16:0 and 20:5ω3/22:6ω3) were above 1 in all populations, indicating secondary heterotrophs following an omnivorous, diatom-origin diet. Whale δ13C values were similar to krill δ13C from productive upwelling areas or the marginal sea-ice zone. No evidence of departure from a high-fidelity Antarctic krill diet was found in any population. Observed inter-population variability was attributed to differences at lower trophic levels rather than dietary shifts.
38701928.limitations: Observational, cross-sectional design limits causal inference. Data are from a single year (2019), preventing assessment of temporal dietary trends. Sample sizes per population are not stated in the abstract, making statistical power difficult to evaluate. No blinding is applicable or reported (wildlife observational study). The finding of no current dietary departure does not preclude future change, and the authors note that projected cryosphere reductions could yet impact feeding ecology. Trophic position estimates carry inherent assumptions about isotopic baselines that cannot be fully assessed from the abstract alone.

37144326.background: Marine-derived n-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LC PUFAs) — EPA and DHA — are associated with beneficial health effects in humans and horses. Krill oil (KO), extracted from Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba), is documented as a safe and bioavailable supplement in humans and several animal species, but evidence regarding its use as a dietary ingredient specifically for horses is lacking. This study aimed to investigate whether KO supplementation could raise EPA and DHA levels in horse red blood cell (RBC) membranes, as expressed by the n-3 index.
37144326.design: Population: Five non-working Norwegian cold-blooded trotter horse geldings (mean body weight 567 ± 38 kg). Intervention: Dietary supplementation with krill oil at 10 mL per 100 kg body weight. Duration: 35 days. Design: Longitudinal (uncontrolled, single-arm) study. Outcome measures: RBC membrane fatty acid profile (including n-3 index), haematology, and serum biochemistry, assessed via blood sampling every 7 days. No control/placebo group is mentioned in the abstract.
37144326.findings: KO was well accepted by all horses with no adverse health effects observed over the 35-day period. RBC membrane n-3 index increased markedly from Day 0 to Day 35 (0.53% vs. 4.05% of total RBC fatty acids). Sum of EPA and DHA increased significantly (p < 0.001), as did total n-3 fatty acids (p < 0.001). n-6 fatty acids were significantly reduced (p < 0.044), resulting in a significantly lower n-6:n-3 ratio by Day 35 (p < 0.001).
37144326.limitations: Key caveats: Very small sample size (n=5), limiting statistical power and generalisability. No placebo or control group reported, making it impossible to rule out confounding from diet or seasonal variation. Study design is longitudinal/observational rather than randomised controlled. Horses were non-working geldings of a single breed, restricting applicability to other horse types or working animals. Blinding status is not mentioned. Overall quality read: low-level evidence (uncontrolled pilot/longitudinal study); results are preliminary and hypothesis-generating only.

36899692.background: Atlantic salmon aquaculture requires adequate dietary phospholipids (PL) during early life stages to support growth and organ health. As fishmeal levels in commercial feeds decline, identifying suitable alternative PL sources — including krill meal (KM), soy lecithin, and marine PL from fishmeal — is important for optimising pre-smolt performance and resilience during the physiologically demanding freshwater-to-seawater transition.
36899692.design: Atlantic salmon (starting weight ~74 g) were fed one of six experimental diets during a freshwater (FW) phase until ~158 g: three krill meal doses (4%, 8%, 12%), a 2.7% fluid soy lecithin diet, a 4.2% marine PL diet, and a control diet. Each PL-supplemented diet was formulated to deliver ~1.3% added PL, comparable to a base diet with 10% fishmeal. Fish were then transferred to a common seawater (SW) tank with crowding stress and fed a commercial diet until ~787 g. Key outcomes were growth, hepatosomatic index (HSI), and liver and gill histology. Exact n per group and blinding details are not reported in the abstract.
36899692.findings: A trend (no statistically significant values reported) toward increased weight gain with higher KM doses was observed in the FW phase but not across the whole trial. The 2.7% soy lecithin diet tended to reduce growth over the whole trial. A trend for decreased HSI with increasing KM dose was seen at transfer but not over the whole trial. Soy lecithin and marine PL diets showed similar HSI to the control over the whole trial. No major differences in liver histology were detected among the control, 12% KM, soy lecithin, or marine PL groups at transfer. A minor positive trend in gill health (lamella inflammation and hyperplasia scores) favoured the 12% KM and control diets over the soy lecithin and marine PL diets at transfer.
36899692.limitations: Results are described as trends with high variability, and no p-values, confidence intervals, or formal statistical significance thresholds are reported in the abstract, limiting interpretability. Sample sizes per dietary group are not stated. The study transitions fish to a common commercial diet in seawater, which may dilute or confound detection of carry-over FW dietary effects. Blinding of assessors for histology is not mentioned. As a single-species controlled feeding trial, generalisability to commercial-scale conditions is uncertain. Overall quality read: controlled experimental trial, but underpowered or highly variable results and limited statistical reporting reduce confidence in the observed trends.

36878083.background: Long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFA) are important for brain development and functioning. Cross-sectional studies have reported positive associations between fish consumption (a key LCPUFA source) and school grades in adolescents, yet no trials had investigated whether LCPUFA supplementation directly improves school performance in this age group. This study aimed to fill that gap by examining both baseline associations between the Omega-3 Index (O3I) and grades, and the effect of one year of krill oil supplementation on academic outcomes.
36878083.design: Double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled trial with repeated measurements in typically developing adolescents with a low O3I at baseline. Two cohorts received krill oil or placebo: Cohort 1 received 400 mg EPA+DHA/day for the first 3 months, then 800 mg EPA+DHA/day for the remaining 9 months; Cohort 2 received 800 mg EPA+DHA/day immediately. O3I was measured via finger prick at baseline, 3, 6, and 12 months. Outcomes included school grades for English, Dutch, and mathematics, plus a standardised mathematics test at baseline and 12 months. Total sample size is not stated in the abstract.
36878083.findings: The krill oil group showed a small but significant increase in mean O3I at all measured time points; however, very few participants reached the target O3I range of 8–11%. At baseline, a significant association between O3I and English grade was observed, and a trend toward association with Dutch grade was noted. After 12 months, no significant associations between O3I and any grade were found. No significant effect of krill oil supplementation was detected on English, Dutch, or mathematics subject grades, or on the standardised mathematics test score. No specific effect-size statistics or p-values are reported in the abstract.
36878083.limitations: The study was undermined by substantial participant dropout and non-adherence, meaning results must be interpreted with caution. Few participants reached the intended target O3I range, limiting the ability to test the intervention at its intended biological dose. The total sample size is not stated in the abstract, precluding a judgment on statistical power. Although the design is a double-blind RCT (highest study quality tier), the adherence and dropout issues substantially reduce internal validity. The dose escalation design across two cohorts adds complexity that may confound comparisons.

36851466.background: Long-chain omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids — EPA and DHA — are associated with health benefits, whereas the shorter-chain plant-derived ALA is not. Dogs fed standard dry commercial diets may have low EPA+DHA status. This study aimed to compare three omega-3 sources (krill meal, fishmeal/oil, and flaxseed cake) for their ability to raise the Omega-3 Index (O3I) — a measure of EPA+DHA in red blood cell membranes — in dogs.
36851466.design: Canine intervention study; n=45 dogs divided into three groups. All dogs were fed premium dry food for 38 days to establish a baseline O3I. Groups were then switched to raw food containing either: krill meal (416 mg EPA+DHA + 971 mg ALA/day), fishmeal/oil (513 mg EPA+DHA + 1,027 mg ALA/day), or flaxseed cake (1,465 mg ALA + 122 mg EPA+DHA/day). Intervention duration was four weeks. Study design details (blinding, randomisation methodology) are not specified in the abstract.
36851466.findings: Mean baseline O3I across all dogs was low at 1.36%, indicating a need for omega-3 supplementation. After four weeks, the krill group showed a significant increase in O3I (1.36±0.44% to 2.36±0.39%; p<0.001) and the fish group also showed a significant increase (1.35±0.22% to 1.90±0.35%; p<0.001). No significant change in O3I was observed in the flaxseed group. Krill meal produced the largest absolute increase in O3I of the three sources.
36851466.limitations: Study was conducted in dogs only, limiting direct extrapolation to humans. Sample size was relatively small (n=45; ~15 per group). Blinding and randomisation procedures are not described in the abstract. The intervention lasted only four weeks, so longer-term effects are unknown. The flaxseed group received a substantially higher ALA dose but very little preformed EPA+DHA, making it a test of ALA conversion efficiency rather than a strictly dose-matched comparison. Overall quality read: small controlled animal trial with limited methodological transparency.

36669014.background: The Omega-3 Index (O3I) — red blood cell (RBC) EPA plus DHA expressed as a percentage of total RBC fatty acids — is a validated biomarker of omega-3 status in humans. However, little is known about O3I status in dogs and cats, despite omega-3 fatty acids having recognised health benefits in these species. This study aimed to fill that gap by developing a practical method to estimate O3I in companion animals.
36669014.design: Cross-sectional study in companion animals recruited from a community veterinary clinic. Random blood samples were collected from 33 dogs and 10 cats (n = 43 total). Paired dried blood spot (DBS) and RBC samples were analysed for fatty acid composition. Intervention dose and duration are not applicable — this was an observational derivation study, not an intervention trial. No blinding or control group is described.
36669014.findings: For both dogs and cats, the R² between the DBS EPA + DHA value and the O3I exceeded 0.96 (p < 0.001), indicating that O3I can be accurately estimated from DBS samples alone. A notable species-comparison finding was that the O3I in dogs and cats was roughly 75% lower than values typically observed in humans. The authors conclude that DBS-based O3I estimation is feasible and that DBS convenience should facilitate future omega-3 research in companion animals.
36669014.limitations: The sample size is small (33 dogs, 10 cats), limiting generalisability across breeds, ages, and diets. The cat group is particularly underpowered. As a cross-sectional, observational derivation study — not a randomised controlled trial — no causal inferences can be drawn. No dietary intake data, supplement use, or health status details are reported in the abstract. Blinding is not mentioned. The equations are derived from a single community clinic, which may not represent the broader companion-animal population. External validation in independent cohorts is not described.

34959789.background: Both omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (n-3 PUFAs) and choline have been individually linked to sports performance, yet their combined effects when co-delivered via krill oil during power training protocols had not been characterised. The study aimed to fill this gap by examining n-3 PUFA and choline profiles following high-intensity exercise after a period of krill oil supplementation.
34959789.design: Thirty-five healthy, well-conditioned power training athletes participated. The intervention group received 2.5 g/day of Neptune Krill Oil™ (providing 550 mg EPA/DHA and 150 mg choline); the control group received olive oil as placebo. The trial ran for 12 weeks using a randomised, double-blind design. Outcomes were assessed after a one-hour high-intensity workout session, with exercise tests conducted at two time points.
34959789.findings: After 12 weeks, only the krill oil group showed a significant increase in HS-Omega-3 Index (4.82% to 6.77%) and a marked reduction in the ARA/EPA ratio (50.72% to 13.61%) (both p < 0.001). Choline concentration recovery between the end of the first and the start of the second exercise test was significantly greater in the krill oil group versus placebo (p = 0.04). Post-exercise total antioxidant capacity declined by 8% in the krill oil group compared with 21% in the placebo group, though this difference did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.35).
34959789.limitations: The sample size is small (n = 35 total), limiting statistical power, particularly for secondary outcomes such as antioxidant capacity where the between-group difference was not significant. The abstract does not report individual group sizes or dropout rates. While the design is randomised and double-blind (moderate-to-good quality), the use of olive oil as placebo — itself a bioactive lipid — could confound comparisons. Study duration and population are specific (12 weeks, power athletes), restricting generalisation to other exercise modalities or populations. No direct performance outcomes are reported in the abstract.

34579064.background: Global demand for high-quality dietary protein is rising, creating a need to identify and evaluate new protein sources. This study investigated whether krill protein hydrolysate could serve as a viable protein source by comparing its acute postprandial amino acid (AA) bioavailability against two established benchmarks: whey protein isolate and soy protein isolate.
34579064.design: Randomized, placebo-controlled, four-arm crossover trial in 10 healthy young males. On four non-consecutive days, participants consumed either water (control) or one of three protein-matched supplements: whey protein isolate, soy protein isolate, or krill protein hydrolysate. Blood samples were taken at baseline and at intervals up to 180 minutes post-consumption. Serum AA concentrations were measured via 1H NMR spectroscopy; hunger and satiety were assessed with visual analogue scales (VAS). Specific doses are not stated in the abstract.
34579064.findings: Whey and krill both produced significantly higher serum AA concentrations than soy in early postprandial windows (whey: 20–60 min; krill: 20–40 min post-consumption). AUC analyses showed whey yielded the highest overall postprandial concentrations of essential AAs (EAAs) and branched-chain AAs (BCAAs), followed by krill, then soy. The authors conclude krill protein hydrolysate is superior to soy protein isolate for raising postprandial EAA and BCAA levels.
34579064.limitations: The sample is very small (n=10), limiting statistical power and generalisability. Participants were restricted to healthy young males, so findings may not apply to other populations (e.g., older adults, females). The observation window was short (180 min), capturing only acute responses. Specific protein doses are not reported in the abstract. As a crossover design it controls for inter-individual variation, but carry-over effects cannot be fully excluded. No blinding status is described, representing a potential quality concern. Overall quality read: small, short-term RCT crossover; findings are preliminary.

34444996.background: Obesity drives metabolic overload and chronic organ inflammation in tissues such as adipose tissue and the liver. While krill oil (KrO) is a source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) and has been associated with health benefits, its effects on obesity-associated inflammation were considered largely unclear. Crucially, no long-term KrO treatment studies had been conducted prior to this work, motivating investigation of its effects during sustained obesogenic feeding.
34444996.design: Animal study (species not specified in abstract; implied rodent model). Sample size not stated in abstract. Intervention: 3% (w/w) KrO supplementation added to a high-fat diet (HFD) with fat primarily from lard, compared to an HFD control group (no KrO). Duration: 28 weeks. Outcomes assessed in epididymal white adipose tissue (eWAT) and liver, including fatty acid and oxylipin profiles, inflammatory pathway markers, adipocyte morphology, and plasma metabolic markers (leptin, adiponectin, HOMA-IR).
34444996.findings: KrO increased EPA, DHA, and associated anti-inflammatory oxylipins (18-HEPE, RvE2, 14-HDHA) in eWAT and liver, while decreasing arachidonic acid and its pro-inflammatory oxylipins (HETEs, PGD2, PGE2, PGF2α, TXB2). In eWAT, KrO activated adipogenesis regulators (PPARγ, CEBPα, KLF15, STAT5A), shifted adipocyte size distribution toward smaller cells, increased total adipocyte numbers (hyperplasia), reduced crown-like structures, and suppressed TNFα and CCL2/MCP-1 inflammatory signaling. Plasma leptin decreased and adiponectin increased over time; insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) improved. In the liver, KrO suppressed IL-1β and M-CSF inflammatory pathways and deactivated REL-A/p65-NF-κB signaling, with increased PPARα protein expression and a trend toward increased IkBα, without affecting liver histology. No specific p-values or numerical magnitudes for these outcomes are reported in the abstract.
34444996.limitations: Study design is a controlled animal experiment; translatability to humans is uncertain. Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. Blinding status is not mentioned. As a preclinical study, causal mechanisms inferred from tissue-level observations require further validation. The abstract notes a trend (rather than significant effect) for IkBα increase, suggesting some outcomes may not reach statistical significance. The 3% w/w dose and lard-based HFD model may not reflect typical human dietary conditions.

33232912.background: Observational studies suggest a link between omega-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (n-3 LCPUFA) and depression in adolescents. However, no supplementation trials had specifically examined whether n-3 LCPUFA could improve depressive feelings in adolescents drawn from the general (non-clinical) population, leaving a gap this study aimed to fill.
33232912.design: One-year double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled krill oil supplementation trial in Dutch adolescents, split into two cohorts. Cohort I began at 400 mg EPA+DHA/day, escalating to 800 mg/day after three months; Cohort II started directly at 800 mg EPA+DHA/day. Total sample size is not stated in the abstract. The Omega-3 Index was tracked via finger-prick blood tests. Depressive symptoms (CES-D) and self-esteem (Rosenberg Self-Esteem scale) were assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months.
33232912.findings: Neither intention-to-treat analyses nor analyses using change in Omega-3 Index as the predictor revealed significant effects on CES-D (depressive symptoms) or RSE (self-esteem) scores after one year of krill oil supplementation. No specific effect sizes, p-values, or group-level scores are reported in the abstract.
33232912.limitations: The authors explicitly flag poor adherence and participant drop-out as key caveats, urging cautious interpretation of the null findings. Quality read: this is a well-designed RCT (double-blind, placebo-controlled, one year) which is a study-type strength, but the exact sample size is unreported in the abstract, and adherence issues likely reduced statistical power, potentially masking a true effect. Generalisability is limited to general-population adolescents in the Netherlands.

33022896.background: The Omega-3 Index measures EPA and DHA in red blood cell membranes as a percentage of total fatty acids and is used as a marker of long-chain omega-3 status. Flaxseed oil (FSO), a plant-based source of the short-chain omega-3 ALA, is commonly promoted in pet feed as an omega-3 source, implicitly assuming ALA is efficiently converted to EPA and DHA. This study was conducted to directly test that assumption in dogs by comparing FSO against a marine long-chain source (astaxanthin krill oil, AKO).
33022896.design: Parallel-group supplementation study in 20 adult Alaskan Huskies of both sexes. One group (n=10) received 1,155 mg EPA/DHA daily from astaxanthin krill oil (AKO); the other (n=10) received 1,068 mg ALA daily from flaxseed oil (FSO). Duration was 6 weeks, with fatty acid and Omega-3 Index measurements taken at baseline (week 0), week 3, and week 6. Blinding status is not reported in the abstract.
33022896.findings: AKO supplementation significantly increased the mean Omega-3 Index from 1.68% at baseline to 2.7% after 6 weeks (p<.0001), with EPA and DHA concentrations rising significantly. Conversely, FSO supplementation led to a significant decrease in the mean Omega-3 Index from 1.6% at baseline to 0.96% at week 6 (p<.0001), with EPA and DHA concentrations falling significantly. These results indicate that ALA from flaxseed oil does not effectively serve as a precursor to EPA and DHA in dogs.
33022896.limitations: The sample size is small (n=10 per group), limiting statistical power and generalisability. The study duration was only 6 weeks. Blinding and randomisation procedures are not described in the abstract. The study used a single dog breed (Alaskan Huskies), so findings may not apply to other breeds. No control (unsupplemented) group is mentioned. Overall quality read: small, short, unblinded (blinding unclear) controlled comparative trial; findings are directionally strong but should be replicated in larger, more diverse canine populations.

33015116.background: Choline is an essential nutrient important for normal muscle function, and free serum choline is known to drop during prolonged high-intensity exercise — a decline that may impair athletic performance. Despite this, few nutritional interventions to counteract exercise-induced choline depletion have been tested in real-world (field) conditions. This study examined whether pre-race supplementation with phosphatidylcholine from krill oil could prevent the expected fall in serum choline and related metabolites during actual triathlon competitions.
33015116.design: Exploratory randomised controlled field study. Participants: 47 triathletes (12 female, 35 male; ages 25–61) competing in either the Ironman-distance Norseman Xtreme Triathlon or the Sprint/Olympic-distance Oslo Triathlon. Intervention: 24 athletes received 4 g/day SuperbaBoost™ krill oil for 5 weeks pre-race; 23 received 4 g/day mixed vegetable oil (placebo). Blood samples were taken pre-race, immediately post-race, and the day after the race to measure serum choline, betaine, and dimethylglycine (DMG).
33015116.findings: Serum choline fell significantly from pre-race to finish in all races, with a greater drop in the Ironman-distance event (34% decrease) than the Sprint/Olympic-distance event (15% decrease). Betaine also decreased, while DMG remained stable across all time points. The krill oil group had significantly higher average serum choline (9.4% higher) and DMG (21.4% higher) than the placebo group. Krill oil supplementers also showed a significantly greater increase in serum choline immediately following race completion, suggesting krill oil may help sustain circulating choline during endurance competition.
33015116.limitations: Exploratory field study design limits causal inference and control over confounding variables (e.g., nutrition, pacing, race conditions). Sample size is modest (n=47 total), which may limit statistical power, particularly for subgroup analyses by race type or sex. The abstract does not report specific p-values beyond noting significance. Blinding status of participants and assessors is not described in the abstract. The study spans two different race formats and distances, introducing heterogeneity. Findings are preliminary and the authors' own characterisation is "exploratory."

32966338.background: Prolonged endurance exercise is known to alter common biomarkers, and competition athletes sometimes require medical assistance or hospitalisation during such events. Clinicians and on-site medical teams may misinterpret exercise-induced biomarker changes as pathological. This study aimed to characterise the magnitude of changes in clinically used biomarkers following an extreme-distance triathlon to guide appropriate clinical interpretation in this context.
32966338.design: Two cohorts of triathletes were studied: participants in the Norseman Xtreme Triathlon — an Ironman-distance event with 5,200 m accumulated climb (n = 98) — and, for comparison, participants in the Oslo Olympic Triathlon (n = 15). Blood samples were collected at three time points: before the race, immediately after, and the morning following each event. No intervention or dose was applicable; this was an observational study. Duration of exposure reflects race completion time, not stated explicitly in the abstract.
32966338.findings: After the Norseman, clinically significant elevations were observed immediately post-race in WBC (14.2 ×10⁹/L), CK (2,450 U/L), and NT-proBNP (576 ng/L) (all p < 0.001). The following day, CRP (39 mg/L) and AST (142 U/L) were markedly elevated (both p < 0.001). Subclinical changes were also noted in haemoglobin, thrombocytes, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, creatinine, ALT, and thyroxine. After the Olympic triathlon, statistically significant but less clinically important changes were seen in the same markers (e.g., CK 303 U/L, NT-proBNP 77 ng/L, CRP 2 mg/L, AST 31 U/L).
32966338.limitations: Observational design limits causal inference; no control group with matched rest. The Olympic triathlon comparator group was small (n = 15), reducing statistical power for that comparison. Blinding was not applicable or described. Individual race completion times and fitness levels, which could influence biomarker responses, are not reported. Baseline health status of participants is not detailed. Overall quality is moderate: prospective observational cohort with repeated measures, reasonable Norseman sample size, but limited comparator group and no randomisation.

32801569.background: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) — specifically EPA and DHA — are associated with health benefits in dogs. The lipid form in which omega-3s are delivered (phospholipid vs. triglyceride) may influence their bioavailability and incorporation into tissues. This study aimed to compare the efficacy of omega-3 fatty acids delivered as phospholipids (via krill meal) versus triglycerides (via fish oil) in raising the omega-3 profile in red blood cells (RBCs) of dogs.
32801569.design: Parallel-group controlled trial in 20 adult Alaskan Huskies (both sexes), split into two groups of 10. One group received 1.7 g/day EPA+DHA from krill meal (phospholipid form); the other received 1.7 g/day EPA+DHA from fish oil (triglyceride form). Duration was 6 weeks, with fatty acid profiles and omega-3 index measured in RBCs at weeks 0, 3, and 6.
32801569.findings: Both groups started with a mean omega-3 index of 3.9%. After 6 weeks, the krill meal group rose to 6.3% vs. 4.7% in the fish oil group (p<0.001). EPA in RBCs drove this difference, increasing from 1.84% to 4.42% in the krill group vs. 1.90% to 2.46% in the fish oil group (p<0.001). Omega-6 PUFAs (arachidonic acid and linoleic acid) were more reduced in RBC membranes in the krill meal group, and the omega-6 to omega-3 ratio was significantly more reduced in the krill meal group than the fish oil group.
32801569.limitations: Small sample size (n=10 per group) limits statistical power and generalisability. The study was conducted exclusively in Alaskan Huskies, a specific working-dog breed, so findings may not extend to other dog breeds or species. Blinding status is not reported in the abstract. The study duration was only 6 weeks, leaving longer-term effects unknown. No clinical health outcomes were assessed — only biochemical RBC markers. The abstract does not report whether randomisation or allocation concealment was used. Overall quality read: small, short-duration, likely unblinded controlled trial in a single breed.

32716968.background: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) are ligand-gated ion channels and key targets for neonicotinoids — chemicals widely used against ectoparasites, arthropod vectors, and agricultural pests. Growing concerns exist about adverse effects on beneficial non-target insects. Prior ex-vivo work had only produced arthropod nAChRs from α subunits alone (Drosophila) or hybrid/chimeric receptors pairing species-specific α subunits with vertebrate β subunits. No fully arthropod-specific nAChR containing both α and β subunits from the same target species had been expressed ex-vivo, leaving a gap in understanding neonicotinoid pharmacology in arthropod pest species.
32716968.design: Species: Lepeophtheirus salmonis (salmon louse, a marine arthropod ectoparasite). Model system: Xenopus laevis oocytes (ex-vivo expression). Intervention: Genes encoding nAChR α and β subunits plus three ancillary proteins (RIC-3, UNC-50, UNC-74) were identified in the L. salmonis genome, cloned via RACE-PCR into an expression vector, and cRNA injected into oocytes. Pharmacological testing: seven neonicotinoids and four non-neonicotinoid compounds tested on the reconstituted receptors. Sample size (n), specific doses, and duration of experiments are not reported in the abstract. Study design is experimental/in vitro (ex-vivo electrophysiology).
32716968.findings: Co-expression of all three ancillary proteins (RIC-3, UNC-50, UNC-74) was essential for successful reconstitution of non-hybrid receptors. Two functional heteropentameric nAChRs were identified: Lsa-nAChR1 (subunits α1, α2, β1, β2), forming one distinct receptor, and Lsa-nAChR2 (subunits α3, β1, β2), forming receptors with two distinct characteristics. Of seven neonicotinoids tested, six acted as partial agonists of Lsa-nAChR1, while only three acted as partial agonists of Lsa-nAChR2. All four non-neonicotinoid compounds had no effect on either receptor. This represents the first reported ex-vivo expression of fully functional, non-hybrid arthropod nAChRs containing both α and β subunits from a single arthropod species.
32716968.limitations: Study is ex-vivo/in vitro, limiting direct extrapolation to in vivo pharmacology or whole-organism toxicology. Sample sizes (number of oocytes/replicates) are not stated in the abstract. Specific doses, EC50 values, or quantitative electrophysiological parameters are not reported in the abstract. The Xenopus oocyte expression system may not fully replicate the native synaptic environment of L. salmonis. Findings are from a single arthropod species, so generalisability to other arthropods (including beneficial insects) is inferential. Overall quality read: mechanistic proof-of-concept study; no blinding or control group in the clinical-trial sense; strength lies in molecular characterisation rather than statistical power.

32650825.background: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a commonly used treatment against sea lice (Lepeophtheirus salmonis) infestations in salmonid aquaculture. Resistance to H2O2 has emerged in some L. salmonis populations. Catalase — an enzyme that degrades H2O2 — had previously been proposed as a resistance marker based on elevated expression and activity in resistant lice. This study aimed to validate catalase as a resistance marker and to identify additional candidate genes associated with H2O2 resistance.
32650825.design: Laboratory study using two L. salmonis strains: one H2O2-sensitive and one H2O2-resistant (P0 generation, unexposed for several years). These were batch-crossed to produce an F2 cohort spanning a wide range of H2O2 sensitivities. F2 adult females were exposed to H2O2 to separate sensitive and resistant individuals. P0 lice and field-collected resistant lice (with prior field H2O2 exposure) were also included. All groups underwent RNA sequencing to identify differentially expressed genes. Sample size (n) is not specified in the abstract.
32650825.findings: Catalase was upregulated in resistant lice following H2O2 exposure compared to sensitive lice, but was not upregulated in unexposed resistant P0 lice, suggesting its expression is exposure-induced rather than constitutive. Five genes were consistently differentially expressed in resistant lice regardless of exposure history: three upregulated (a DNA polymerase family gene, a Nesprin-like protein gene, and an unannotated small-protein-encoding gene) and two downregulated (endoplasmic reticulum resident protein 29 and aquaporin Glp1_v2). Glp1_v2, an aquaglyceroporin, was highlighted as the most promising candidate, potentially reducing H2O2 influx through lower channel expression.
32650825.limitations: No sample sizes (n) are reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. The study is a controlled laboratory cross (F2 generation) combined with field-collected lice, so generalisability across wild populations is unclear. The reliance on RNA sequencing provides expression-level evidence only; functional validation of the five candidate genes is not reported. As an observational/experimental transcriptomic study without blinding described, causal inference is limited. Overall quality read: moderate — hypothesis-generating, with no dose information or effect sizes provided in the abstract.

32269236.background: Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) are a cornerstone of the Antarctic food web, and their lipid reserves are critical for their own health and the survival of their predators. Krill lipids are largely diet-derived, originating from phytoplankton such as diatoms and flagellates. Despite this dependency, few studies have linked spatial and temporal variation in krill lipid composition to variation in food availability. Remotely-sensed environmental data offer large-scale coverage of potential food supply, but linking these to krill biochemistry had previously only been done at small scales with limited samples.
32269236.design: The study used fishery-derived krill samples collected over 3 years in Antarctic/Southern Ocean waters. Sample size (n) is not stated in the abstract. No experimental intervention or dose was involved; this was an observational study. Krill lipid and fatty acid data were paired with remotely-sensed satellite data: chlorophyll a (Chl-a) as a proxy for phytoplankton food availability, and sea surface temperature (SST). Within-year and between-year variation in both environmental and biochemical parameters were examined.
32269236.findings: Chl-a levels were positively associated with krill total lipid levels, with triacylglycerol showing a particularly strong positive relationship. Plankton-derived fatty acid biomarkers in krill — including n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (n-3 PUFAs) — increased with decreasing SST and increasing Chl-a. The study demonstrates that combining satellite remote-sensing with krill biochemical data is a useful approach for exploring physiological and biological relationships between krill and their Southern Ocean environment. Specific effect sizes, p-values, and percentages are not reported in the abstract.
32269236.limitations: Exact sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. Krill samples were fishery-derived rather than from controlled or randomised collection, introducing potential spatial and temporal sampling biases. The study is observational and correlational in design, precluding causal inference. No blinding is applicable given the non-experimental nature. Relationships between satellite-derived surface measurements and actual krill diet in deeper waters may be imprecise. Overall quality read: observational, multi-year, large-scale — a moderate-quality ecological study limited by opportunistic sampling.

32252833.background: Atlantic salmon aquaculture relies heavily on fishmeal, but alternative protein sources are urgently needed, especially during the finishing feeding period when feed demand is highest and effects on flesh quality are most pronounced. This study examined whether Antarctic krill meal could substitute fishmeal without compromising fish health or fillet quality.
32252833.design: Population: growing Atlantic salmon (~2.3 kg at start, ~3.9 kg at end); n not explicitly stated but quadruplicate sea cages per diet were used. Two isoprotein (35%) and isolipid (35%) diets compared: a low fishmeal diet (FM, 15% fishmeal) vs. a krill meal diet (KM, 12% krill meal substituting fishmeal). Duration: 3 months. Design: controlled comparative feeding trial in sea cages (quadruplicate per diet); not described as blinded.
32252833.findings: Final body weight was similar between groups (~3.9 kg). The KM group showed: more voluminous body shape, leaner hearts, and improved fillet integrity, firmness, and colour. Ectopic epithelial cells and focal calcium deposits in the intestine were detected only in the FM group, indicating better gut health in the KM group. Liver transcriptome (microarray) revealed upregulation of immune genes and structural genes (cadherin, connexin) in KM fish. Skeletal muscle showed upregulation of myosin heavy chain protein genes. Scanning electron microscopy and FTIR spectroscopy indicated more ordered and stable collagen architecture in the KM group. NEFA profiles suggested altered n-3, n-6 and SFA metabolism in KM fish.
32252833.limitations: Exact sample size (number of fish per cage) is not reported in the abstract. The study is a single controlled feeding trial without blinding described, limiting generalisability. Duration is relatively short (3 months). No statistical values (p-values, effect sizes) are provided in the abstract. Findings are associative; mechanistic conclusions are inferred from transcriptomic and morphological data. As a single industry-relevant species trial in sea cages, results may not translate directly to other production contexts or krill meal inclusion levels.

31652561.background: Choline is an essential, water-soluble nutrient required for neurotransmitter synthesis, cell membrane integrity, and numerous other biological processes. Dietary intake alone may not fully meet the body's choline requirements, making supplements relevant. Because choline exists in structurally different forms across sources, its absorption, metabolism, and ultimate bioavailability may vary considerably. This study was conducted to compare how two commercially available choline sources — phosphatidylcholine (as found in SuperbaBoost™ krill oil) and choline bitartrate salt — differ in their pharmacokinetic profiles for free choline and key choline metabolites.
31652561.design: The study was conducted in healthy volunteers. The abstract does not specify the total sample size (n), exact doses administered, or study duration. Participants received a single dose of either SuperbaBoost™ krill oil (providing choline as phosphatidylcholine) or choline bitartrate. Plasma kinetics of free choline and selected metabolites (betaine, dimethylglycine/DMG, and trimethylamine N-oxide/TMAO) were measured over time. The design appears to be a comparative pharmacokinetic study; blinding and randomisation details are not stated in the abstract.
31652561.findings: Overall plasma levels of free choline were comparable between SuperbaBoost™ krill oil and choline bitartrate. However, peak choline concentration was reached significantly later following SuperbaBoost™ intake, indicating a slower absorption profile. The metabolite pattern differed meaningfully: betaine and dimethylglycine (DMG) — considered biologically beneficial metabolites — were higher with SuperbaBoost™, while trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) — a metabolite associated with potential cardiovascular concern — was substantially lower with SuperbaBoost™ compared to choline bitartrate. Specific numerical values (Tmax, Cmax, AUC, p-values) are not reported in the abstract.
31652561.limitations: Key pharmacokinetic parameters (Cmax, Tmax, AUC), exact doses, sample size, and study duration are not provided in the abstract, limiting quantitative appraisal. The study used a single-dose design, so findings may not reflect steady-state or long-term supplementation effects. Quality caveats: study type is described only as a pharmacokinetic study in healthy volunteers; blinding, randomisation, and crossover vs. parallel-group design are unspecified. As a single-dose, likely small-cohort study, generalisability to broader or clinical populations is uncertain. The abstract does not address potential confounders such as background diet.

31451724.background: Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) are a keystone species in the Southern Ocean, yet their physiological responses to climate change remain poorly understood. Ocean acidification — driven by absorption of atmospheric CO₂ into surface waters — is known to alter lipid biochemistry in some marine organisms, with potential cascading effects up the food chain. This study investigated whether projected near-future and extreme elevated pCO₂ levels would affect krill lipid content and fatty acid composition, including markers of immune function and cell membrane fluidity.
31451724.design: Laboratory exposure experiment using adult Antarctic krill. Sample size not stated in the abstract. Krill were exposed to five pCO₂ treatments: one ambient control (400 μatm) and four elevated levels (1000, 1500, 2000, and 4000 μatm), spanning near-future to extreme ocean acidification scenarios. Duration was one year, with assessments conducted across seasons (summer, winter, and spring). No information on blinding or replication numbers is provided in the abstract.
31451724.findings: Total lipid mass (mg g⁻¹ dry mass) was unaffected by near-future pCO₂ (up to 2000 μatm). Fatty acid composition and immune- and membrane-fluidity-related fatty acid ratios were similarly unaffected at near-future levels, except for an increase in 18:3n-3/18:2n-6 ratios at 1500 μatm in winter and spring. Extreme pCO₂ (4000 μatm) had no effect in summer, but in winter and spring it elevated 18:2n-6 (up to +1.2%), 20:4n-6 (up to +0.8%), lowered 18:3n-3/18:2n-6 and 20:5n-3/20:4n-6 ratios, and increased phospholipid/sterol ratios up to three-fold, indicating increased membrane fluidity.
31451724.limitations: Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. The study is a single controlled laboratory experiment, which may not fully replicate natural Southern Ocean conditions or seasonal food availability. Only adult krill were tested; other life stages may respond differently. The 4000 μatm extreme treatment exceeds near-future projections and may have limited ecological relevance. No p-values or confidence intervals are reported in the abstract. Blinding and randomisation details are not described. Overall quality read: controlled laboratory study, long duration (1 year) is a strength, but unknown replication and single life-stage focus are notable caveats.

31327902.background: Choline and its metabolites play multiple physiological roles including muscle function, memory, methylation reactions, and hepatic lipid transport. Despite this importance, it was unclear whether dietary phosphatidylcholine (PC) derived from Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) could meaningfully raise plasma choline and related metabolite concentrations in dogs. This study was conducted to test that hypothesis against a non-supplemented control group.
31327902.design: Parallel-group controlled trial in 20 adult Alaskan Huskies (both sexes; n=10 per group). The intervention group received a diet containing 8% krill meal (as a source of PC) for 6 weeks; the control group received no krill meal supplementation. Blood samples were collected at baseline and at 6 weeks and analysed for plasma choline and its metabolites. Dose of isolated PC not separately stated; krill delivered via dietary inclusion.
31327902.findings: After 6 weeks, the krill-supplemented group showed a significantly greater increase in plasma choline compared to controls (mean increase 4.53 µmol/L vs. 1.21 µmol/L; p=0.014). Supplemented dogs also showed significantly more pronounced increases in betaine (p<0.001), dimethylglycine (p<0.01), trimethylamine-N-oxide (TMAO; p<0.001), and trimethyllysine (p<0.001). Significant correlations were observed between changes in choline and changes in each of its metabolites.
31327902.limitations: Small sample size (n=20 total; n=10 per group) limits statistical power and generalisability. The study population is a specific working-dog breed (Alaskan Huskies), so applicability to other dogs or humans is unclear. Blinding status is not reported in the abstract. The krill was delivered as whole krill meal rather than purified PC, so effects of other krill components cannot be excluded. Duration was short (6 weeks). The notably significant rise in TMAO — a metabolite with cardiovascular risk associations in some species — is not discussed in the abstract. Study type is a small controlled animal trial of moderate-to-low quality.

31151199.background: Long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFAs) are considered important for brain development and function, particularly during adolescence. Observational studies have linked fish consumption (a dietary LCPUFA source) to better cognition in adolescents, but intervention trials were lacking. This study aimed to fill that gap by testing whether krill oil supplementation could improve cognitive performance in adolescents with a low Omega-3 Index (O3I ≤ 5%).
31151199.design: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled supplementation trial with repeated measurements in Dutch adolescents (n = 267 randomized). Two cohorts were used: Cohort I received 400 mg EPA+DHA/day (krill oil) or placebo; Cohort II received 800 mg EPA+DHA/day (krill oil) or placebo. Duration was 12 months, with assessments at baseline (T0), 3 months (T1), 6 months (T2), and 12 months (T3). Omega-3 Index was monitored via finger-prick blood samples at all time points; a neurocognitive test battery was administered at T0, T2, and T3.
31151199.findings: Krill oil supplementation produced a small but statistically significant increase in mean Omega-3 Index; however, few participants reached the intended target O3I range of 8–11%. No significant effect of krill oil supplementation on any neurocognitive test outcomes was detected. Similarly, no significant relationship was found between O3I levels and neurocognitive test scores in covariate-corrected mixed models using either condition or O3I as predictors.
31151199.limitations: The achieved increase in O3I was small in most participants, likely due to non-compliance, meaning the intended omega-3 status target was rarely reached — possibly explaining the null cognitive findings. No specific effect sizes, p-values, or cognitive subtest results are reported in the abstract. Study quality is relatively high (double-blind RCT with placebo control and repeated measures), but the effective biological dose delivered may have been insufficient. The authors call for further research; generalisability may be limited to low-O3I adolescent populations.

30456311.background: Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) occupy a keystone ecological role in the Southern Ocean as the primary prey of Antarctic predators. Declining krill abundance could trigger a major ecological regime shift. Ocean acidification — driven by anthropogenic CO2 emissions altering seawater chemistry — is predicted to progress most rapidly at high latitudes, yet little was known about how it might affect krill physiology and survival.
30456311.design: Laboratory exposure study using adult Antarctic krill. Sample size not stated in the abstract. Krill were incubated under near-future ocean acidification conditions of 1,000–2,000 μatm pCO2 for one year. No further detail on control conditions, blinding, or replication is provided in the abstract.
30456311.findings: Adult krill exposed to elevated pCO2 (1,000–2,000 μatm) for one year were able to survive, grow, store fat, mature, and maintain normal respiration rates. They also actively regulated the acid-base balance of their body fluids under elevated pCO2 conditions, a mechanism identified as key to their resilience. No specific mortality rates, growth figures, or p-values are reported in the abstract.
30456311.limitations: Study was conducted in a controlled laboratory setting, which may not fully replicate natural Southern Ocean conditions. Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. The study focuses exclusively on adults; larval or juvenile stages may respond differently. No blinding or control condition details are described. As a single-lab experimental study, generalisability to wild populations and multi-stressor scenarios (e.g., warming combined with acidification) is uncertain.

30312832.background: Intense endurance exercise in sled dogs triggers systemic inflammation and muscle damage. Omega-3 fatty acids (particularly in phospholipid form from krill) are known to have anti-inflammatory properties, but their effect on exercise-induced inflammation in canine athletes competing in ultra-endurance events had not been studied. This study aimed to assess whether dietary krill meal supplementation could attenuate post-race inflammation and muscle damage in Iditarod sled dogs.
30312832.design: Field study involving two teams of sled dogs competing in the 2016 Iditarod race. One team (n=16) received 8% krill meal supplementation for 5 weeks prior to race start; the other team (n=16) served as an unsupplemented control. Blood samples were analysed in race finishers only (10 treatment, 11 control dogs) for omega-3 index, C-reactive protein (CRP, inflammation marker), and creatine kinase (CK, muscle damage marker). No blinding or randomisation details are reported.
30312832.findings: Krill meal-fed dogs had a significantly higher omega-3 index at race start (6.2% vs. 5.2% in controls; p<.001). Post-race CRP rose from 4.26 to 16.56 μg/ml in the supplemented group versus 7.05 to 37.04 μg/ml in controls, a significant between-group difference (p<.001). Post-race CK increased from 99.55 to 515.69 IU/l (supplemented) and from 90.75 to 715.90 IU/l (controls), but this difference was not statistically significant (p=.266), though a numerical tendency toward less muscle damage was observed in the krill group.
30312832.limitations: Uncontrolled field study with very small sample sizes (10–11 finishers per group); only one team per condition, precluding true randomisation. Confounders including race speed, musher skill, fitness level, and diet composition beyond krill supplementation cannot be excluded. No blinding is described. The study's own authors acknowledge results require confirmation in more controlled trials. Quality read: low-controlled, non-randomised field study, n very small, high risk of confounding.

29549496.background: Depression is common among adolescents, and long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFAs) have been suggested to play a role in mental health. However, research specifically in adolescents is limited, and the relationship between LCPUFAs and self-esteem has never previously been studied. This study aimed to explore associations between blood fatty acid profiles — including EPA, DHA, Omega-3 Index (O3I), n-6 docosapentaenoic acid (Osbond acid, ObA), n-3 DPA, and arachidonic acid (AA) — and both depression and self-esteem in adolescents attending lower general secondary education (LGSE).
29549496.design: Baseline cross-sectional data were drawn from a krill oil supplementation trial in adolescents attending LGSE who had an O3I ≤ 5%. Sample size is not stated in the abstract. Fatty acids and O3I were measured in whole blood. Depression was assessed using the Centre for Epidemiologic Studies Depression (CES-D) scale and self-esteem via the Rosenberg Self-Esteem (RSE) scale. Bayesian regression models (BayesFactor package in R) were used to evaluate associations. No intervention dose or trial duration is reported for this baseline analysis.
29549496.findings: Depression scores indicative of depression (CES-D ≥ 16) were found in 29.4% of participants. Extreme Bayesian evidence (BF10 = 245) was found for a weak negative association between ObA and depression score (β = −0.16; 95% credible interval −0.28 to −0.04). Substantial evidence (BF10 = 4) was found for a weak positive association between ObA and self-esteem score (β = 0.09; 95% CI −0.03 to 0.20). No evidence was found for associations of DHA, EPA, or O3I with either depression or self-esteem. When all fatty acids were modelled together, all 95% credible intervals included zero, indicating no significant associations.
29549496.limitations: The cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. The study is limited to adolescents with low O3I (≤ 5%), restricting generalisability. Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting quality appraisal. As a baseline analysis from a supplementation trial, it is observational only, with no blinding relevant to this component. The self-esteem–LCPUFA association for ObA, while showing substantial Bayesian evidence, had a 95% credible interval crossing zero. Overall quality read: cross-sectional, Bayesian observational analysis, unknown sample size, no blinding — preliminary and hypothesis-generating only.

29389317.background: Manual handling of microliter volumes in micro-electromembrane extraction (μ-EME) is error-prone and can compromise microextraction performance. This study aimed to develop a semi-automated liquid-handling set-up to improve repeatability and accuracy of μ-EME, and to achieve exhaustive (complete) extraction of basic drugs from biological fluids including human urine and plasma.
29389317.design: Laboratory analytical methods study (no clinical population). Model analytes were three basic drugs: nortriptyline, papaverine, and haloperidol. A syringe pump was directly connected to a disposable microextraction unit via flexible polymeric tubing to form a three-phase μ-EME system. Donor solution: 1.3 µL of 10 mM HCl; free liquid membrane (FLM): 2.5 µL of 1-ethyl-2-nitrobenzene; acceptor solution: 1.3 µL of 25 mM HCl. Extraction was driven by 150 V d.c. for 10 minutes. Matrices tested included salt- and protein-containing standard solutions, human urine, and human plasma. Sample size (n) is not reported in the abstract. Extracts were analysed by capillary electrophoresis with UV detection (CE-UV).
29389317.findings: Exhaustive extraction of all three drugs was achieved under optimised conditions. Extraction recoveries ranged from 79% to 102% across all tested matrices (standard solutions, human urine, and human plasma). The combined μ-EME-CE-UV method showed good linearity (coefficients of determination ≥ 0.992), high repeatability (RSD ≤ 6.5%), and limits of detection ≤ 0.15 mg/L. Excellent repeatability and accuracy of the automated withdrawal sequence were reported for solution volumes of 1–5 µL, with strong correlation between withdrawn and collected volumes.
29389317.limitations: This is a proof-of-concept analytical methods development study, not a clinical trial, so findings reflect controlled laboratory conditions rather than real-world diagnostic performance. Sample size (number of replicates or biological samples) is not stated in the abstract, limiting quality assessment. No blinding is applicable or reported. The study evaluates only three model basic drugs, so generalisability to other compound classes is unknown. Relevance to krill-oil research is absent — this paper concerns drug extraction methodology rather than lipid or omega-3 science.

28107816.background: The omega-3 index (erythrocyte membrane EPA + DHA as % of total fatty acids) is a standard biomarker for long-chain omega-3 intake. When it changes, the relative fractions of other membrane fatty acids must also shift. This study investigated how a krill-oil-derived omega-3 phospholipid preparation affects the full erythrocyte fatty acid profile over time — including 22 fatty acids beyond EPA and DHA — and examined downstream effects on plasma endocannabinoid-type mediators, in a metabolically compromised non-human primate model.
28107816.design: Population: 6 diabetic, dyslipidemic non-human primates (n=3 treatment, n=3 vehicle control). Intervention: escalating oral doses of an omega-3-rich phospholipid preparation extracted from krill oil — 50, 150, and 450 mg phospholipids/kg/day — each dose level administered for 4 weeks (12 weeks total treatment), followed by an 8-week washout. Vehicle and test item delivered daily by gavage. Blood sampled at baseline then bi-weekly. Erythrocyte fatty acids measured by methylation/GC; plasma endocannabinoids by LC-APCI-MS. Controlled animal study.
28107816.findings: Treatment produced dose-related increases in erythrocyte membrane EPA and DHA fractions, and dose-related decreases in other polyunsaturated fatty acids, particularly omega-6 PUFAs. Saturated fatty acid concentrations in erythrocytes remained unchanged throughout. Plasma endocannabinoid-type mediators followed the same pattern: arachidonic-acid-derived mediators decreased, palmitic- and oleic-acid-derived mediators were unchanged, and an EPA-derived downstream metabolite increased. The ratio of unsaturated to saturated fatty acids in erythrocyte membranes was therefore unaltered; only relative unsaturated (especially omega-6) fractions changed.
28107816.limitations: Very small sample size (n=3 per group), limiting statistical power and generalisability. Non-human primate model may not fully translate to humans. All animals were diabetic and dyslipidemic, restricting applicability to healthy populations. No blinding details reported in the abstract. The abstract reports directional trends but provides no specific numerical values (e.g., exact % changes, p-values). Overall quality read: exploratory, hypothesis-generating animal study; findings should be interpreted cautiously pending larger, controlled human trials.

28095913.background: High LDL-cholesterol is a major cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factor, typically managed with statins. The role of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids in modulating circulating cholesterol remains inconclusive in the literature. This study evaluated a novel krill oil-derived preparation of omega-3-rich phospholipids (predominantly phosphatidylcholine) on multiple CVD risk factors in a naturally occurring dyslipidemic and type-2 diabetic animal model.
28095913.design: Non-human primate study; n=6 dyslipidemic animals with naturally occurring type-2 diabetes (3 vehicle control, 3 treatment). The krill oil phospholipid preparation was administered daily by oral gavage at escalating doses of 50, 150, and 450 mg phospholipids/kg/day, each for 4 weeks, over a 12-week total duration. Blood lipids, apolipoproteins, omega-3 index, and diabetes, inflammatory, and safety biomarkers were measured at varying intervals. No statistical analyses were performed due to small group sizes.
28095913.findings: At the lowest dose (50 mg/kg/day), only HDL-cholesterol and apolipoprotein A1 increased. At intermediate and high doses (150 and 450 mg/kg/day), total cholesterol, LDL-cholesterol, apolipoprotein B100, and triglycerides decreased, while HDL-cholesterol and apolipoprotein A1 increased. No effects were observed on diabetes markers, inflammatory markers, or safety biomarkers. Specific numerical values for lipid changes are not reported in the abstract.
28095913.limitations: Very small sample size (n=3 per group) precluded any statistical analysis, making it impossible to assess significance or generalisability. This is a non-human primate study, limiting direct translation to humans. Single-arm dose escalation within each animal prevents separation of dose and time effects. Blinding status is not reported. Results are presented as control-adjusted means for lipids only. Authors acknowledge findings are preliminary and require confirmation before human trials.

27622510.background: Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) is an important and abundant fishery resource with harvest levels expected to grow. A significant proportion of krill escape through commercial trawl mesh during fishing operations. Estimating total fishing mortality — including mortality of individuals that escape the gear — is a critical component of sound fisheries management, yet methods for quantifying escape mortality in krill remain poorly developed.
27622510.design: Study population: Antarctic krill caught by commercial trawl. Sample size not stated in the abstract. Intervention: a covered codend sampling technique was used to capture krill that would otherwise escape through 16 mm mesh nets, followed by onboard holding-tank observations to monitor post-escape survival over time. Study duration not specified in the abstract. Design: observational field study with survival modelling; no control group or blinding described.
27622510.findings: Escape mortality was estimated at 4.4 ± 4.4%, indicating krill are relatively tolerant of the capture-and-escape process. Haul duration, hydrological conditions, maximum fishing depth, and catch composition had no significant effect on escape mortality. Holding-tank conditions also introduced no additional mortality. A non-parametric Kaplan-Meier analysis modelled mortality over time. There was a weak, non-significant tendency for smaller individuals to experience higher mortality than larger individuals.
27622510.limitations: Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting assessment of statistical power. The study examined only one mesh size (16 mm), so findings may not generalise to other gear configurations. No p-values or confidence intervals beyond the ±4.4% range are provided. Holding-tank conditions may not perfectly replicate the natural post-escape environment. Study type is a single observational field study without blinding or randomisation, placing it at a relatively low level of evidence. The non-significant size-mortality trend warrants further investigation with larger samples.

27401364.background: The influence of omega-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (n-3 LCPUFAs) on brain function remains debated. Positive effects have been reported in children with learning difficulties, cognitively impaired elderly, and depressed individuals, but results across studies are mixed. Observational data in adolescents link fish consumption (a source of n-3 LCPUFAs) to better academic achievement, yet controlled intervention studies in typically developing adolescents are absent. This trial was designed to fill that gap by testing whether raising the Omega-3 Index via krill-oil-derived EPA+DHA supplementation improves cognition, academic achievement, and mental well-being in healthy adolescents.
27401364.design: Double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled trial (Food2Learn). Population: 264 typically developing adolescents aged 13–15 years attending lower general secondary education, selected for a low baseline Omega-3 Index (<5%). Cohort I (n=130) received 400 mg/day EPA+DHA; Cohort II (n=134) received 800 mg/day EPA+DHA (or matched placebo); duration: 52 weeks. Doses were adjusted at 3 months to target an Omega-3 Index of 8–11%. Outcomes assessed at baseline, 6 and 12 months included a neuropsychological test battery, questionnaires, and a standardised math test; school grades were also collected. Sub-samples underwent sleep monitoring (n=64) and eye-tracking (n=33).
27401364.findings: This abstract describes a study protocol only (registered as pre-results). No outcome data, effect sizes, or statistical results are reported. The abstract does not provide any findings on cognition, academic achievement, behaviour, visual processing, or Omega-3 Index changes in response to supplementation.
27401364.limitations: As a protocol paper, no efficacy or safety results are available for evaluation. Key methodological caveats to note for future data interpretation: the dose-adjustment design at 3 months adds complexity; sub-sample sizes for sleep (n=64) and eye-tracking (n=33) are small. The overall sample (n=264) is moderate. The study is double-blind and placebo-controlled, which is a methodological strength. Restriction to a single school type (lower general secondary education) and a single country (Netherlands) may limit generalisability. The source of EPA+DHA is described as a supplement but krill oil is referenced in the title; the abstract does not explicitly confirm the lipid vehicle.

27112112.background: Liposomal drug formulations can improve solubility, enable targeting, and provide controlled release of poorly water-soluble drugs. A key requirement is that the drug remains associated with the carrier during vascular transit. Existing in vitro release tests often fail to predict behaviour of lipophilic drugs realistically, because such compounds tend to stay in bilayers even after dilution. In vivo, however, rapid drug loss to endogenous lipophilic sinks (lipoproteins, plasma proteins, red blood cell and endothelial membranes) is commonly observed after i.v. injection. A biomimetic, mechanistically informative in vitro assay was therefore needed.
