27112112.design: In vitro study (no human or animal subjects). The assay used donor liposomes loaded with model drugs incubated with large multilamellar liposomes as a biomimetic acceptor sink, separated by flow field-flow fractionation (FlFFF). Model compounds were p-THPP (structurally related to the clinical photosensitizer temoporfin), Sudan II (clogP 5.45), Sudan III (clogP 6.83), Sudan IV, Oil Red O, and Sudan Black. Variables included donor-to-acceptor lipid mass ratios (1:1 to 1:10), liposome size, and lamellarity. Drug amounts in donor and acceptor fractions were quantified at distinct incubation time points. Sample size (n) and specific concentrations/doses are not reported in the abstract.
27112112.findings: Drug transfer kinetics from donor to acceptor liposomes was rate-determining relative to intramembrane redistribution, and the overall process was adequately described by a single first-order kinetic model. A correlation was established between donor-to-acceptor lipid mass ratio and transfer rate, enabling scaling to physiological ratios. Sudan II and Sudan III showed very rapid transfer and considerable release to the water phase. For more lipophilic compounds, transfer rates ranked: Sudan IV (fastest) > Oil Red O > Sudan Black > p-THPP (slowest). At an equimolar donor-to-acceptor lipid mass ratio, transfer half-lives ranged from ~12 min (Sudan IV) to ~1.5 h (p-THPP). The assay provided mechanistic insights and more realistic predictions of in vivo drug loss than conventional in vitro release tests.
27112112.limitations: This is an in vitro physicochemical study with no in vivo validation arm reported in the abstract, so direct confirmation of physiological predictive accuracy is not demonstrated. Biological sinks are modelled only by multilamellar liposomes; interactions with actual plasma proteins, lipoproteins, or cell membranes are not directly tested. Sample sizes (n) and statistical analyses are not described in the abstract. As a mechanistic in vitro assay, generalisability to diverse drug classes or clinical liposomal formulations beyond the tested model compounds is uncertain. Overall quality read: non-clinical, exploratory/proof-of-concept design; no blinding applicable.

26950903.background: Liposomes are used to solubilise poorly water-soluble drugs for intravenous administration. Despite good in-vitro stability, premature drug loss after i.v. injection can occur via transfer to plasma proteins and cell membranes. This study refines a 2014 in-vitro predictive tool (Hinna et al.) designed to model this drug transfer behaviour using donor and acceptor liposome systems, aiming to enable simultaneous quantification of both drug transfer and drug release to the aqueous phase — a capability lacking in the earlier method.
26950903.design: In-vitro laboratory study; no human or animal subjects. Small drug-loaded donor liposomes were incubated with large acceptor liposomes (serving as biological sink models) and separated by asymmetrical flow field-flow fractionation (AF4) with on-line UV/VIS detection and off-line HPLC. Model compound was p-THPP, a structural analogue of the clinical photosensitiser temoporfin. Samples were taken at specific incubation time points to derive transfer and release kinetics. The AF4 separation exposed samples to a dilution factor of approximately 600. Sample size (n) is not reported in the abstract.
26950903.findings: The refined AF4 method achieved near-baseline separation of donor and acceptor vesicles and reliable quantification of drug content in both fractions, enabled by improved size-homogeneity, colloidal stability, and reduced turbidity of acceptor liposomes. Transfer of p-THPP to acceptor vesicles followed first-order kinetics with a half-life of approximately 300 minutes. Equilibrium distribution between donor and acceptor vesicles was proportional to lipid mass ratio. An initial rapid transfer of ~5% p-THPP was observed. During AF4 separation itself, donor and acceptor phases separated within a few minutes, with only minor transfer (≤2%) detectable within the AF4 channel, supporting method validity.
26950903.limitations: This is an in-vitro physicochemical study; biological relevance to actual in-vivo drug transfer (e.g., to plasma proteins or cell membranes) is inferred but not directly tested. Only one model compound (p-THPP) was evaluated. No sample size (n) or replicate information is provided in the abstract. No blinding is applicable to this analytical method study. Generalisability to nanocarriers other than liposomes requires a sufficient size difference between carrier and acceptor phase, which may not always be achievable. Overall quality read: proof-of-concept method development study; useful for hypothesis generation but limited in direct clinical extrapolation.

26729157.background: The relationship between omega-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (LCPUFAs) and cognitive performance remains heavily debated. This study aimed to investigate whether blood omega-3 status is associated with cognitive performance in typically developing adolescents, a population where such relationships are not yet well established.
26729157.design: Cross-sectional analysis of baseline data from Food2Learn, a double-blind randomised placebo-controlled krill oil supplementation trial. Population: 266 typically developing Dutch adolescents aged 13–15 years. The Omega-3 Index was measured via finger-prick blood sample. Cognition was assessed using a neuropsychological battery (LDST, D2 attention test, Digit Span Forward and Backward, Concept Shifting Test, Stroop test). Multiple regression analyses were conducted with covariate correction. Intervention dose and trial duration are not reported in the abstract, as only baseline (pre-supplementation) data were used.
26729157.findings: The mean Omega-3 Index was 3.83% (SD 0.60), indicating generally low omega-3 status. Of nine cognitive outcome parameters tested, two showed statistically significant associations with the Omega-3 Index: (1) Letter Digit Substitution Test score (β = 0.136, p = 0.039), suggesting higher information processing speed with a higher Omega-3 Index; and (2) errors of omission on the D2 attention test (β = −0.053, p = 0.007), suggesting less impulsivity with a higher Omega-3 Index. No significant associations were found for the remaining seven cognitive parameters.
26729157.limitations: Being a cross-sectional baseline analysis, causality cannot be established. Only 2 of 9 cognitive measures were significant, raising the possibility of chance findings without correction for multiple comparisons (not mentioned in abstract). The sample is restricted to typically developing Dutch adolescents aged 13–15, limiting generalisability. Quality read: the parent study is a double-blind RCT, lending methodological rigour, but this analysis is observational (cross-sectional); n = 266 is moderate. No correction for multiple testing is reported in the abstract.

26260413.background: Marine omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) are recognised for their health benefits, but fish oil (FO) and krill oil (KO) differ structurally: in FO, EPA and DHA are primarily esterified to triacylglycerols (TAGs), whereas in KO they are primarily esterified to phospholipids. This study aimed to investigate whether these structural differences translate into distinct effects on lipid content and fatty acid distribution across lipid classes in the liver and brain.
26260413.design: Mouse feeding study; n = 6 per group (3 groups total: high-fat diet control, high-fat + FO, high-fat + KO). Specific doses of FO and KO are not stated in the abstract. Duration: 6 weeks. Liver and brain lipid extracts were analysed using shotgun and TAG lipidomics. Statistical analysis used Student's t-test following log-transformation.
26260413.findings: Both FO and KO more than doubled liver TAG content versus control. Both oils decreased 18:2 and 20:4 fatty acids and increased 20:5 and 22:6 in liver and brain. Key differences: 18:2 decreased across all lipid classes in FO but only minimally in KO. In KO-fed mice, liver ceramides and diacylglycerols were decreased and cholesteryl esters increased; in FO-fed mice, liver plasmalogens were decreased. In the brain, diacylglycerols were decreased by both oils (more so by KO); ceramides and lactosylceramides were increased more by FO than KO. Hepatic sphingolipid and 20:4 changes were greater with KO, suggesting stronger anti-inflammatory action and enhanced insulin sensitivity relative to FO.
26260413.limitations: Study conducted in mice only, limiting direct translation to humans. Sample size is very small (n = 6 per group), reducing statistical power. Doses of FO and KO are not reported in the abstract, preventing dose-response assessment or direct comparison of equivalent EPA/DHA intakes. No blinding or randomisation details are described. As an animal study without a control non-high-fat arm, the independent contribution of the high-fat dietary background cannot be fully disentangled. Overall quality read: exploratory animal lipidomics study; small n; no blinding reported — results should be considered hypothesis-generating.

25961320.background: Krill oil (KO) and related krill products have been evaluated across a range of animal models, but no consolidated synthesis of their mechanisms of action and comparative effects existed. This review was conducted to summarise findings from animal studies on KO, krill powder (KP), and krill protein concentrate (KPC), and to provide insight into how the phospholipid delivery form of omega-3s may differ from triglyceride forms in terms of tissue uptake and gene expression.
25961320.design: This is a narrative review of animal studies; no single population, sample size, intervention dose, or study duration applies. Animal models covered include obesity, depression, myocardial infarction, chronic low-grade inflammation, and ulcerative inflammation. The review also compares KO to KP and KPC formulations. No original trial data are reported; specific n values, doses, and durations are not stated in the abstract.
25961320.findings: KO, KP, and KPC demonstrated effects across multiple disease-relevant animal models (obesity, depression, myocardial infarction, and inflammatory conditions). The phospholipid form of EPA and DHA found in krill products influences tissue uptake differently compared to the triglyceride form, and this delivery difference has a differential impact on gene expression profiles. The review identifies clinical health segments with high potential for krill-product-specific benefits warranting further investigation; specific numerical results are not reported in the abstract.
25961320.limitations: As a narrative review, this paper is subject to selection bias and lacks the rigour of a systematic review or meta-analysis. No original data are presented, so quality assessment of individual studies (blinding, randomisation, sample sizes) cannot be drawn from the abstract alone. The review is limited to animal studies, restricting direct translation to human clinical outcomes. No quantitative effect sizes, p-values, or doses are provided in the abstract.

28962346.background: Krill powder (Superba™) is used as a dietary supplement, but formal preclinical safety data were needed to characterise any potential toxicological risks before broader use. This study was conducted to establish a no observed adverse effect level (NOAEL) for krill powder under subchronic dietary exposure conditions.
28962346.design: Species: rats (strain not specified in abstract). Intervention: dietary inclusion of Superba™ krill powder at 9.67% (w/w) vs. control diet. Duration: 13 weeks (subchronic). Sample size: not stated in abstract. Both sexes included. Design: controlled dietary study; randomisation/blinding details not reported in abstract.
28962346.findings: No differences in body weight or food consumption were observed in either sex. Clinical chemistry differences were detected in krill-powder-treated animals but were deemed toxicologically non-significant. A statistically significant decrease in absolute (but not relative) heart weight was observed in both sexes; no corresponding histological changes were found. Hepatocyte vacuolation was noted histologically in males, without other indicators of hepatic dysfunction. The NOAEL was established at 9.67% krill powder in the diet — the only dose tested.
28962346.limitations: Only a single dose level (9.67% w/w) was tested, preventing dose-response characterisation; the NOAEL is therefore an estimate based on the tested dose alone. Sample size is not reported in the abstract, limiting power assessment. The study is a preclinical animal (rat) model, so direct translation to human safety is uncertain. Blinding and randomisation procedures are not described. As a subchronic rodent toxicity study it represents moderate-quality preclinical evidence, adequate for regulatory screening but not a substitute for human clinical safety data.

25315197.background: Obesity is associated with impaired glucose tolerance and insulin resistance. Omega-3-rich supplements such as fish oil (FO) and krill oil (KO) have been proposed to improve metabolic outcomes, but their comparative effects on glucose handling in an obese animal model warranted direct investigation. This study aimed to evaluate whether FO or KO supplementation could improve glucose tolerance and related metabolic gene expression in experimentally induced obese rabbits.
25315197.design: Animal study using 24 male New Zealand white rabbits randomly divided into four groups (n=6 per group inferred): KO-castrated (KO-treated), FO-castrated (FO-treated), C-castrated (non-treated obese control), and NC-non-castrated (non-treated lean control). Castration was used to induce obesity. Specific doses of KO and FO are not stated in the abstract. Treatment duration was 2 months, after which an intravenous glucose tolerance test (IVGTT) was performed in all animals.
25315197.findings: Fasting blood glucose concentrations were significantly lower in both FO- and KO-treated groups compared with the castrated untreated control (C). During IVGTT, FO-treated rabbits returned to baseline blood glucose by 30 minutes, while KO-treated rabbits did so by 60 minutes. In the liver, Cpt2 and Hmgcs2 gene expression were significantly increased in FO-fed rabbits vs. C; Acaca expression was significantly reduced in both KO- and FO-fed rabbits. In skeletal muscle, Hmgcs2 and Cd36 were significantly higher in KO-fed rabbits vs. C, while Acaca expression was significantly lower in both KO- and FO-fed rabbits vs. C. Authors attributed improvements to ameliorated insulin sensitivity/secretion and modified β-oxidation and lipogenesis gene expression.
25315197.limitations: Animal study only (New Zealand white rabbits); findings may not translate directly to humans. Total sample size is small (n=24; approximately 6 per group), limiting statistical power. Specific doses and formulations of FO and KO are not reported in the abstract, preventing dose-response assessment or direct dosage comparison. No blinding is described. The obesity model relies on castration, which may not fully replicate human metabolic obesity. Study type (controlled animal experiment) is preclinical; quality read: moderate for animal research but not directly applicable to human supplementation guidelines.

28962289.background: Krill oil is an emerging marine lipid supplement, but its safety profile required formal preclinical evaluation. This study was conducted to assess the potential toxicological and genotoxic risks of Superba™ krill oil, addressing regulatory needs for establishing safe intake levels before broader use.
28962289.design: Species: rats (strain not specified in abstract). Population size: not stated. Intervention: dietary administration of krill oil at varying doses (including a 5% krill oil diet) versus control diets. Duration: 13 weeks (subchronic toxicity arm). Additional genotoxicity arm: bacterial mutagenicity assay (Ames-type test implied, though not explicitly named). Specific dose levels beyond 5% are not detailed in the abstract.
28962289.findings: No treatment-related differences in body weight, food consumption, or functional observation battery parameters were observed in either sex. Haematological and clinical chemistry differences were noted in krill-oil-treated groups but were deemed toxicologically non-significant. Significant decreases in absolute and covariant heart weight were found in some krill-oil-treated animals, with no corresponding histological changes. Periportal microvesicular hepatocyte vacuolation was observed histologically in males fed 5% krill oil, without other indicators of hepatic dysfunction. The NOAEL was established at 5% krill oil. Genotoxicity testing showed no mutagenicity in bacteria.
28962289.limitations: Key limitations include: sample size (n) and specific dose groups are not reported in the abstract, limiting quantitative interpretation. The study is a standard preclinical animal study and does not directly inform human safety. Blinding status is not mentioned. The bacterial genotoxicity assay, while standard, does not capture all genotoxic mechanisms. The clinical relevance of the heart weight finding and hepatocyte vacuolation remains uncertain. Overall quality read: regulatory-style preclinical study — moderate-to-good methodological rigor implied by NOAEL determination, but detail in the abstract is limited.

25105960.background: Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba) are harvested by trawlers using a variety of gear designs, yet very little is known about the size selectivity of these gears. Understanding size selectivity — a trawl's ability to catch or release different sizes of individuals — is considered crucial for managing a sustainable krill fishery.
25105960.design: The study combined morphological description of krill with the mathematical modelling software FISHSELECT to simulate size selectivity across diamond meshes ranging from 5–40 mm with mesh opening angles of 10–90°. Simulation outputs were validated against experimental trawl hauls and underwater video imaging of mesh geometry during active fishing. Sample size (n) and specific trawl haul details are not stated in the abstract.
25105960.findings: Contrary to the initial expectation that krill would encounter netting in random orientations (due to high towing speeds and krill swimming behaviour), results indicated that size selectivity is a well-defined process in which krill consistently encounter meshes at an optimal orientation for escapement. Simulation-based predictions were supported by experimental trawl data and video observations. The study produced size-selectivity predictions across a range of netting configurations relevant to the commercial krill fishery. No specific numerical selectivity estimates (e.g. L50 values) are reported in the abstract.
25105960.limitations: No specific numerical results (e.g. selection curves, L50, or SR values) are provided in the abstract, limiting detailed appraisal of findings. Sample size and experimental replication details are not reported. The study relies heavily on model simulations (FISHSELECT), with experimental trawl hauls serving primarily as validation rather than as an independent experimental design. As a simulation-plus-validation study, it is not a controlled randomised trial, which constrains the strength of causal inference. Generalisability may be limited by the specific mesh types and angle ranges tested.

24834104.background: Marine-derived oils rich in omega-3 long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) are associated with reduced plasma lipids and anti-inflammatory effects. Krill oil (KO) is a newer marine oil whose EPA and DHA are incorporated mainly into phospholipids (PLs) rather than triacylglycerols (TAG), unlike conventional fish oil (FO). This study aimed to compare how FO and KO differentially regulate lipid-related gene expression and plasma/liver lipid profiles in a high-fat diet mouse model.
24834104.design: Male C57BL/6J mice were fed one of three diets for 6 weeks: a high-fat diet (HF; 24% fat, primarily lard and soy oil), HF supplemented with FO (5.8% FO replacing part of the lard), or HF supplemented with KO (5.7% KO replacing part of the lard). Sample size (n per group) is not stated in the abstract. Plasma and liver lipids (cholesterol, TAG, PLs, fatty acid composition) were measured, and gene expression was assessed by quantitative PCR in liver and intestinal epithelium. This was a controlled dietary intervention study in an animal model.
24834104.findings: FO significantly decreased plasma cholesterol (esterified and unesterified), TAG, and PLs, attributed at least partly to reduced VLDL content, with subsequent liver lipid accumulation. KO lowered plasma non-esterified fatty acids (NEFA) with only minor hepatic lipid accumulation. Despite a lower dietary omega-3 content in the KO diet, plasma and liver PL omega-3 levels were similar between FO and KO groups, suggesting higher omega-3 bioavailability from KO. KO more efficiently reduced arachidonic acid and its elongation/desaturation products. FO primarily upregulated genes involved in fatty acid metabolism; KO specifically downregulated genes involved in early isoprenoid/cholesterol and lipid synthesis steps.
24834104.limitations: Study was conducted in mice (C57BL/6J), limiting direct extrapolation to humans. The abstract does not report the number of animals per group, reducing ability to assess statistical power. No blinding procedure is described. As an animal feeding study, it is inherently preclinical in nature. The 6-week duration may not reflect long-term effects. Exact omega-3 doses per group are not specified in the abstract, though dietary fat percentages are provided.

24461313.background: Elevated serum triglycerides (TG) are a recognised cardiovascular risk factor. Omega-3 fatty acids are known to reduce TG, but the effect of krill oil specifically had not been well characterised in adults with borderline high or high TG levels (150–499 mg/dL) and low habitual fish intake. This study was conducted to explore whether daily krill oil supplementation could lower fasting serum TG in this at-risk population without adversely raising LDL cholesterol (LDL-C).
24461313.design: Double-blind, randomised, multi-centre, placebo-controlled trial. n = 300 male and female adults with fasting serum TG 150–499 mg/dL and low habitual fish intake. Five parallel arms: placebo (olive oil) or krill oil at 0.5, 1, 2, or 4 g/day. Duration: 12 weeks, with fasting serum lipid measurements at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks. Due to high intra-individual TG variability, data from all four krill oil arms were pooled for a time- and dose-independent one-way ANOVA to assess efficacy.
24461313.findings: Pooled krill oil supplementation produced a statistically significant calculated reduction in fasting serum TG of 10.2% relative to placebo. LDL-C levels were not increased in the krill oil groups compared with placebo. Authors conclude the findings suggest krill oil is effective in reducing a cardiovascular risk factor, though confidence in the results requires further investigation.
24461313.limitations: High intra-individual variability in TG concentrations necessitated pooling all krill oil dose groups, preventing dose-response conclusions. The authors themselves note that more individual measurements per treatment group are needed to increase confidence. While double-blind and placebo-controlled (moderate-to-good design), the pooling approach reduces statistical precision. Specific p-values beyond the characterisation of "statistically significant" are not reported in the abstract. The placebo was olive oil, which has potential bioactive effects of its own. Generalisability is limited to low fish-consuming adults with borderline-to-high TG.

23706001.background: Prior animal studies (Zucker rats, diet-induced obese mice) and a previous human trial showed that dietary DHA and EPA delivered as krill oil reduce peripheral endocannabinoid levels, ectopic fat formation and hyperglycemia in overweight/obese individuals, and that elevated triglycerides may correlate with elevated circulating endocannabinoids. This study extended that work by testing krill powder (which adds protein to krill oil) on triglycerides and endocannabinoid levels in mildly obese men.
23706001.design: Open-label, controlled supplementation study. 11 mildly obese men (mean BMI 32.3 kg/m², mean age 42.6 years, mean plasma triglycerides 192.5 ± 96.3 mg/dL) received krill powder (4 g/day orally) for 24 weeks; 6 subjects served as unsupplemented controls. Outcomes included plasma DHA/EPA, endocannabinoids (anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol), triglycerides, anthropometrics (waist/hip ratio, visceral fat/skeletal muscle mass ratio) and body weight, assessed at baseline, 12 weeks and 24 weeks.
23706001.findings: Krill powder supplementation produced significant increases in total plasma DHA and EPA at both 12 and 24 weeks. Plasma anandamide levels fell by 59% at 12 weeks and 84% at 24 weeks. Plasma triglycerides decreased by 22.5% at 12 weeks and 20.6% at 24 weeks. Waist/hip ratio and visceral fat/skeletal muscle mass ratio were significantly reduced at 24 weeks. No change in body weight was observed. 2-arachidonoylglycerol results are not reported quantitatively in the abstract.
23706001.limitations: Very small sample (n=11 treatment, n=6 control), limiting statistical power and generalisability. The control group was not randomised or blinded — the study appears unblinded and non-randomised, representing a low quality of evidence (pilot/exploratory design). Male-only cohort restricts applicability to other populations. No placebo supplement was used for controls. Dose rationale and krill powder composition details (protein 34%, krill oil 61.8%) are noted but the abstract does not report p-values or confidence intervals beyond directional significance claims.

23203133.background: The biological activities of omega-3 fatty acids (n-3 FAs) have been studied for decades, yet relatively little attention has been paid to how dietary form — triglycerides (TGs), ethyl esters, or phospholipids (PLs) — affects their metabolism and effects. The growing availability of novel marine raw materials such as krill and fish by-products, which deliver n-3 FAs predominantly in PL form, has prompted renewed interest in whether n-3 PLs behave differently from other dietary forms.
23203133.design: This is a narrative review article, not an original interventional or observational study. No specific study population, sample size (n), intervention dose, or trial duration is reported. The authors synthesise emerging evidence on the nutritional properties and metabolic bioavailability of n-3 PLs, with a focus on metabolic syndrome parameters, and compare n-3 PLs to n-3 TGs and ethyl ester forms.
23203133.findings: The review reports that n-3 PLs derived from marine sources (e.g., krill, fish by-products) show distinct metabolic bioavailability compared to n-3 TGs and ethyl esters. Evidence is highlighted suggesting that n-3 PLs have beneficial nutritional properties relevant to multiple parameters of metabolic syndrome. No specific numerical results, effect sizes, or p-values are provided in the abstract.
23203133.limitations: As a narrative review, this paper is subject to selection bias in the literature chosen and lacks the rigour of a systematic review or meta-analysis. No original data, sample sizes, blinding, or controls are reported. The abstract does not specify the number or quality of primary studies reviewed. Conclusions are therefore qualitative and should be interpreted cautiously pending larger, well-controlled clinical trials directly comparing dietary forms of n-3 FAs.

22303341.background: Dietary ω-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) — specifically DHA and EPA — are associated with benefits in glucose and lipid homeostasis and inflammation modulation. Krill oil (KO) delivers these fatty acids in phospholipid form, whereas fish oil (FO) delivers them as triglycerides. This study aimed to compare the hepatic gene-expression effects of these two ω-3 PUFA sources to evaluate whether the molecular form of delivery makes a functional difference in the liver.
22303341.design: Species: mice (young, fed a low-fat diet). Intervention: dietary supplementation with either a phospholipid krill oil fraction (KO) or fish oil (FO) at an equimolar dose of EPA and DHA. Outcome measure: whole-liver transcriptome profiling (gene expression). Exact sample size (n), specific doses, and study duration are not reported in the abstract.
22303341.findings: KO supplementation downregulated pathways involved in hepatic glucose production and lipid/cholesterol synthesis, and appeared to increase mitochondrial respiratory chain activity. FO at an equimolar EPA+DHA dose modulated fewer hepatic pathways than KO and did not affect glucose metabolism, lipid metabolism, or the mitochondrial respiratory chain. Notably, FO upregulated the cholesterol synthesis pathway — the opposite of KO's effect. Neither diet produced measurable changes in plasma lipids, glucose, or insulin levels, attributed to the young age of the animals and their low-fat diet.
22303341.limitations: This was an animal (mouse) study, limiting direct translation to humans. Animals were young and fed a low-fat diet, which likely blunted plasma metabolic outcomes, reducing the ability to detect physiological effects. Exact sample size, specific doses, and study duration are not stated in the abstract. No blinding or randomisation details are described. As a preclinical transcriptomic study, causality and clinical relevance cannot be established; authors themselves note that metabolic-disease models or higher-fat diets may be needed to observe functional effects.

21917191.background: Strong evidence links consumption of fish and fish oil rich in long-chain n-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) to reduced coronary heart disease risk. However, it was unclear whether n-3 LCPUFA delivered via enriched everyday food products (rather than fish oil capsules) produces equivalent bioavailability and cardiovascular benefits. This study aimed to compare the two delivery formats on plasma lipid incorporation and cardiovascular risk markers.
21917191.design: Parallel-group randomised controlled intervention trial in 159 healthy men and women. Four arms: fish pâté (34 g/day, n=44), enriched fruit juice (500 ml/day, n=38), concentrated fish oil capsules (3 capsules/day, n=40), and an unsupplemented control group (n=37). All active interventions delivered approximately 1 g EPA+DHA per day. Duration: 7 weeks. Outcomes included plasma fatty acid composition, serum lipids, and markers of inflammation and oxidative stress.
21917191.findings: Compared with the control group, plasma n-3 LCPUFA levels and the EPA:arachidonic acid ratio increased equally across all three intervention groups, indicating equivalent bioavailability from food products and fish oil capsules. However, no significant changes in blood lipids or markers of inflammation and oxidative stress were observed in any intervention group relative to controls.
21917191.limitations: The study was conducted exclusively in healthy subjects, which likely limited the ability to detect changes in cardiovascular risk markers — a known ceiling effect in low-risk populations. The trial duration was only 7 weeks and the dose (~1 g EPA+DHA/day), while considered cardioprotective, may have been insufficient to move biomarkers in healthy individuals. Sample sizes per arm were modest (n=37–44). The abstract does not specify blinding status. Overall quality: randomised controlled trial with an active comparator and control group, but limited by population selection and short duration.

21749725.background: Elevated endocannabinoid levels are associated with metabolic syndrome, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (ω-3 PUFAs) are known to improve cardiovascular metabolic risk factors. This study investigated whether krill oil (KO), as a dietary ω-3 PUFA source, could modulate the endocannabinoidome — specifically levels of endocannabinoids (anandamide, 2-AG), PPARα-activating congeners (OEA, PEA), and their biosynthetic precursors — across multiple metabolically relevant tissues in a high-fat diet mouse model.
21749725.design: Animal study using high-fat diet (HFD)-fed mice. Exact sample size (n) not stated in the abstract. Intervention: dietary krill oil supplementation at multiple doses (dose-dependent design). Duration: 8 weeks. Tissues analysed included inguinal and epididymal adipose tissue, liver, gastrocnemius muscle, kidneys, and heart. Lipids were quantified via LC-APCI-MS and LC-IT-ToF-MS. Specific KO doses are not reported in the abstract.
21749725.findings: Eight weeks of HFD increased endocannabinoid levels in all tissues except liver and epididymal adipose tissue. KO supplementation reduced anandamide and/or 2-AG levels in all tissues except the liver, generally in a dose-dependent manner. Endocannabinoid biosynthetic precursor levels were also broadly down-regulated by KO, suggesting KO lowers endocannabinoid levels partly by reducing precursor availability. Effects of KO on the PPARα ligands OEA and PEA were present but typically smaller than those on endocannabinoids. Specific numerical values for lipid concentrations or effect sizes are not reported in the abstract.
21749725.limitations: Animal (mouse) study only — translational relevance to humans is uncertain. Exact sample size and specific KO doses are not disclosed in the abstract, limiting quality assessment. No blinding or randomisation details are described. The liver showed no response to either HFD or KO for endocannabinoids, which may reflect tissue-specific confounds not fully explained. Overall quality read: preclinical, mechanistic study; generalisability is restricted, and the absence of reported n and dose details reduces reproducibility appraisal.
